behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is parser/category handoff: rename entries must flow into the rename bucket without double-counting in add/delete buckets. This is more than one local function, but it does not require real OS/network/subprocess behavior if the test feeds representative git change records through wired modules. Rejected higher level (System): behavior text only says "Renamed files appear..." and does not require validating real git process semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Wire parser + categorizer + report builder in-process, feed a fixture that includes rename records, and assert rename count increments while add/delete do not for the same file movement.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Could fail at module handoff; a pure unit test of one helper would miss double-counting across components.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: This behavior targets robustness against real git diff behavior for binary content and CLI execution stability. Real subprocess output/exit behavior and actual repo artifacts are part of the failure mode; mocks can hide decode/format/runtime issues. Rejected higher levels: no AI/ML dependency and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real temp git repo, commit a binary file change, run the actual CLI process, and assert successful exit plus binary file presence in counts/output categories.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Requires real git subprocess + repository state to catch true crash conditions.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: The key failure is handling real git behavior on an unborn repository (often specific exit codes/messages). That boundary is subprocess + repo state, so System is the minimum to catch real failures. Rejected higher levels: no model output dependence and no multi-step agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a real git repo with no commits, invoke the CLI as a subprocess, and assert zero-error exit with an empty structured report.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: In-process stubs of git responses could miss real unborn-HEAD process behavior.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly ties correctness to "actual change history of the repository," which requires exercising real git history semantics and CLI execution over a real repo. Mock history risks false confidence. Rejected higher levels: no AI/ML correctness component, no chained agentic steps.
behaviors.test_description: Build a real repo history with known adds/deletes/renames/modifies across commits, run the CLI, and assert reported counts equal the known ground truth.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The assertion depends on real repository history, which is outside unit/integration-only in-process scope.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: This compares behavior across real git index vs working-tree states, which is a real git/OS/subprocess boundary. Fakes can miss true staged/unstaged semantics. Rejected higher levels: no AI/ML output dependence and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: In a real repo, create equivalent staged and unstaged change scenarios, run the CLI in both modes, and assert byte-for-byte identical structured stdout output.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Requires authentic git staging mechanics; lower levels would not reliably catch index/worktree parity bugs.
